solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    PeakIndex = find_peak(Nums, N),

    SumIncreasing = calculate_sum(Nums, 0, PeakIndex),
    SumDecreasing = calculate_sum(Nums, PeakIndex, N - 1),

    case compare(SumIncreasing, SumDecreasing) of
        greater -> 0;
        less -> 1;
        equal -> -1
    end.

%% Helper to find the peak index
find_peak(Nums, N) ->
    PeakIndex = 0,
    % Iterate until we find the element that is not greater than the previous one.
    % Since the array is bitonic (strictly increasing then strictly decreasing), 
    % the peak is the last element where nums[i] > nums[i-1].
    case lists:foldl(fun(Peak, I, Element) ->
        case I of
            [0] -> Peak; % Should not happen for N >= 3
            _ ->
                PreviousElement = lists:nth(I - 1, Nums),
                CurrentElement = Element,
                if CurrentElement > PreviousElement -> I; true -> Peak
                end
        end
    end, 0, Nums, 1) of
        PeakIndex -> PeakIndex
    end.

%% Simpler way to find peak using standard loop logic emulation (more direct translation of JS logic)
find_peak_alt(Nums, N) ->
    find_peak_loop(Nums, N, 1, 0).

find_peak_loop(_, 1, _, Peak) -> Peak;
find_peak_loop(Nums, N, I, CurrentPeak) ->
    case lists:nth(I, Nums) of
        Element ->
            PreviousElement = lists:nth(I - 1, Nums),
            if Element > PreviousElement ->
                find_peak_loop(Nums, N, I + 1, I);
            true ->
                CurrentPeak
            end
    end.

%% Using the simpler iterative logic based on the problem constraints
find_peak(Nums, N) ->
    find_peak_internal(Nums, N, 1, 0).

find_peak_internal(_Nums, 1, _I, Peak) -> 0;
find_peak_internal(Nums, N, I, CurrentPeak) when I >= N ->
    CurrentPeak;
find_peak_internal(Nums, N, I, CurrentPeak) ->
    PreviousElement = lists:nth(I - 1, Nums),
    CurrentElement = lists:nth(I, Nums),
    if CurrentElement > PreviousElement ->
        find_peak_internal(Nums, N, I + 1, I);
    true ->
        CurrentPeak
    end.


%% Helper to calculate sum over a range [Start, End] inclusive
calculate_sum(Nums, Start, End) ->
    lists:foldl(fun(Acc, Index) ->
        Acc + lists:nth(Index, Nums)
    end, 0, lists:seq(Start, End)).

%% Replacing the potentially complex/slow list access with a fold for efficiency
%% Let's rewrite calculate_sum to be efficient using fold on a sublist
calculate_sum_efficient(Nums, Start, End) ->
    if Start > End -> 0;
    true ->
        Sublist = lists:sublist(Nums, Start, End - Start + 1),
        lists:sum(Sublist)
    end.

% Re-evaluating the logic based on efficiency and correctness.

solve(Nums) ->
    N = length(Nums),
    
    % Find Peak Index: Iterate i from 1 to N-1. Keep updating PeakIndex as long as nums[i] > nums[i-1].
    PeakIndex = find_peak_index(Nums, N),

    % Calculate sums
    SumIncreasing = calculate_sum_efficient(Nums, 0, PeakIndex),
    SumDecreasing = calculate_sum_efficient(Nums, PeakIndex, N - 1),

    % Compare
    case compare(SumIncreasing, SumDecreasing) of
        greater -> 0;
        less -> 1;
        equal -> -1
    end.

find_peak_index(Nums, N) ->
    % Since N >= 3, we can safely start the search.
    PeakIndex = 0,
    find_peak_loop_final(Nums, N, 1, 0).

find_peak_loop_final(_, 1, _I, Peak) -> Peak;
find_peak_loop_final(Nums, N, I, CurrentPeak) when I >= N ->
    CurrentPeak;
find_peak_loop_final(Nums, N, I, CurrentPeak) ->
    % In Erlang, list access is O(i). For N=10^5, this iterative approach might be slow if we don't optimize.
    % However, since we only traverse the array once, O(N) complexity overall is maintained.
    
    % Safely get elements (assuming N >= 2 here since N>=3 constraint applies)

            if CurrentElement > PreviousElement ->

%% find_peak_index/1: Finds the index of the peak element.
% Returns {ok, peak_index} or an error.
find_peak_index(Nums) ->
    case Nums of
        [] -> error(empty_array);
        [_] -> {ok, 0}; % Single element array (though N>=3)
        [A, B] -> % Two element array (must be increasing, peak is B)
            if B > A -> {ok, 1};
            true -> {ok, 0} % If B <= A, it must be strictly decreasing/constant, peak at 0
        _ ->
